introduction: The glaucomas are a group of diseases in which there is gradual loss of vision (actually visual field) due to a form of optic neuropathy. A raised intraocular pressure is a risk factor. It is a common cause of blindness worldwide and once a patient is blind from glaucoma, vision cannot be restored.

features: generally symptomless except in the late stages when symptoms of visual loss present
marked elevation of intraocular pressure with severe pain

investigations: Central and peripheral visual field test.
Gonioscopy to view the angle of the eye

goals: Stop further loss of retinal ganglion cells and nerve fibre layer by prompt treatment.
drug: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors e.g. acetazolamide, dorzolamide
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Timolol - can precipitate heart block, bradycardia and bronchospasms
Pilocarpine - mioses, spasms of accommodation, retinal detachment
Acetazolamide - paraesthesia, renal stone formation, Steven – Johnson syndrome, gastrointestinal disturbances, decreased libido, fatigue, weight loss.
Latanoprost –foreign body sensation, mild conjunctival hyperaemia and increased pigmentation of iris
Timoptol is contraindicated in Asthmatics and patients with heart block.
Acetazolamide is contraindicated in patients with pre-existing systemic acidosis, patients with sickle cell disease and allergy to sulphonamides.
Pilocarpine is contraindicated in eyes with previous retinal detachment, and pathological myopia.

differential_diagnoses: Primary optic atrophy (pallor but no cupping of the disc)
complications: Rubeosis irides with resultant secondary glaucoma
prevention: Screening for glaucoma should be incorporated into the health plan of any nation
definitive_treatment: Peripheral iridectomy or Yag laser iridotomy for acute closed angle glaucoma (to the affected eye as well as the normal fellow eye)
Laser photo-ablation of the ciliary body
